epigraph.text: Two are better than one, because they have a good reward for their toil.
epigraph.quote: The whole is greater than the sum of its parts.

content.text:
When studying two random variables simultaneously, we need 
distributions that describe their joint behavior.

content.title: Joint PMF
content.content:
For discrete random variables $X$ and $Y$, the **joint PMF** is:
\[
 p(x, y) = P(X = x \text{ and } Y = y)
\]
It must satisfy $p(x, y) \geq 0$ and $\sum_x \sum_y p(x, y) = 1$.

content.title: Joint PMF
content.problem:
Two dice are rolled. Let $X$ = first die, $Y$ = second die. The joint 
PMF is $p(x, y) = \frac{1}{36}$ for $x, y \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}$.

content.title: Marginal PMF
content.content:
The **marginal PMF** of $X$ is found by 
summing over all values of $Y$:
\[
 p_X(x) = P(X = x) = \sum_y p(x, y)
\]
Similarly, $p_Y(y) = \sum_x p(x, y)$.

content.title: Joint PDF
content.content:
For continuous random variables $X$ and $Y$, the **joint PDF** 
$f(x, y)$ satisfies:

1. $f(x, y) \geq 0$ for all $(x, y)$

2. $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) \, dx \, dy = 1$

3. $P((X, Y) \in A) = \iint_A f(x, y) \, dx \, dy$

content.title: Marginal PDF
content.content:
The **marginal PDF** of $X$ is:
\[
 f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x, y) \, dy
\]

marginNotes.text:
He is before all things, and in him 
all things hold together.
